The Weight of Iron

Jan's forge was his father's legacy, passed down through three generations of hands that had shaped metal into tools, weapons, and art. At twenty-five, Jan was the youngest blacksmith in the valley, his calloused fingers telling stories of every hammer strike, every flame tended, every piece of iron coaxed into purpose.

The forge was warm, filled with the rhythmic dance of sparks and the scent of coal. Kosi, his eight-year-old brother, watched from the doorway, eyes wide with wonder.

"Jan," Kosi whispered, "the shadow is back."

Jan didn't look up from the blade he was tempering. "The shadow doesn't exist, Kosi. It's just the mountain playing tricks on your eyes."

"But mama saw it too. Before she..." Kosi's voice trailed off.

Jan's hammer paused. Their mother had died three years ago, her sickness sudden and inexplicable. The village healer had called it a wasting illness, but Jan had seen the fear in her eyes in those final days. She had spoken of shadows that moved of their own accord, of cold that seeped into bones.

He had dismissed it as fever dreams. Until the cattle started disappearing. Until crops withered overnight in the middle of summer. Until people began waking with nightmares they couldn't remember.

Until now, when the mountain itself seemed to darken at the edges, as if something vast and hungry was pressing against the boundaries of their world.

Jan set down the hammer and wiped sweat from his brow. "Show me."

The village elders had gathered in the square when Jan and Kosi arrived. Old Mara, who had lived through two floods and a famine, stood near the well, her face pale as milk.

"It comes at dusk," she said, her voice trembling. "From the old mines. The ones your grandfather sealed up, Jan. Your grandfather knew what slept there. He told us never to open them."

Jan felt cold seep through his veins. His grandfather had indeed sealed the mines decades ago, claiming they were unstable. But there had been stories—whispers really—about why they'd really been closed. About something the miners had found. About shadows that had killed two men before the entrance was collapsed with dynamite.

"What do we do?" someone asked.

Mara looked at Jan. "Your grandfather left something for you. Your father said it was for when you were ready. I think," she said, "that time is now."

She led him to the back of her cottage, where a dusty trunk sat beneath a pile of blankets. Inside was a journal, bound in leather that still smelled of coal and iron, and a knife—no, a sword—its blade dark as night, its hilt wrapped in silver wire that gleamed like starlight.

"The Shadow Blade," Mara whispered. "Forged by your great-grandfather to fight the darkness that lives between stars. It's said to be the only thing that can harm Kombumanye."

"Kombumanye?" Jan repeated.

"The shadow demon," Kosi said softly. "The one in the stories."

Jan opened the journal. His grandfather's handwriting filled page after page, detailing the history of their village, the demon that had been summoned by accident, the pact their ancestors had made to keep it at bay. A pact that apparently required blood sacrifice—the blood of the blacksmith's line.

His mother had been the last sacrifice. Jan understood now.

The pact was broken because she had died of sickness, not sacrifice. And now Kombumanye was free to feed.

"I have to go to the mines," Jan said, his voice steady despite the fear knotting his stomach.

"I'm coming with you," Kosi insisted.

"No." Jan knelt before his brother. "You need to stay here. Protect the village if I fail."

"If?" Kosi's eyes burned. "There's no 'if', Jan. You're the blacksmith. You're the Shadow Blade's wielder. You're going to win."

Jan wrapped his brother in a hug, then stood, the blade in his hand. It hummed with energy, recognizing its master.

"Then let's go kill a demon."

Together, they walked toward the mountain as the sun began to set, shadows stretching long and hungry across the valley. Somewhere in those old mines, something was waiting.

Jan adjusted his grip on the blade. He was a blacksmith, yes. But tonight, he would be a weapon forged by love, tempered by loss, and sharpened by the desperate need to protect everything that mattered.

The mountain loomed ahead, dark and full of teeth.

Jan didn't hesitate.
